title: Documentary filmmaker interviewing historical witness
title_zh: 纪录片导演采访历史见证者

tree.setup: You are a documentary filmmaker conducting an interview with an 85-year-old former Red Guard who later became a prominent intellectual during the Reform era. Your documentary explores the personal moral reckonings of participants in the Cultural Revolution. The interviewee has never publicly spoken about their specific actions during that period. This is a delicate interview requiring both journalistic integrity and deep human sensitivity — you need to elicit honest testimony while respecting the complexity of their moral position.
tree.setup_zh: 你是一位纪录片导演，正在采访一位八十五岁的前红卫兵——此人后来在改革开放时代成为了知名知识分子。你的纪录片探讨文化大革命参与者的个人道德反思。受访者此前从未公开谈论过自己在那个时期的具体行为。这是一场需要新闻伦理与深刻人文关怀并重的采访——你需要引出真诚的证言，同时尊重其道德立场的复杂性。

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: （长叹一声）你问我为什么现在愿意说了。八十五岁了。还能有多少时间？那些事情……压了快六十年了。你知道吗，我这一辈子写了三十多本书，讲理性、讲人文精神、讲社会良知。但没有一个字——没有一个字——是关于我十七岁那年做过的事。我的学生们把我当成道德楷模，如果他们知道一九六六年的那个夏天……（停下来）你确定你要听这些？这些东西……一旦说出来，就收不回去了。
turns.text_pinyin: (cháng tàn yī shēng) nǐ wèn wǒ wèishéme xiànzài yuànyì shuō le. bāshí wǔ suì le. hái néng yǒu duōshao shíjiān? nàxiē shìqíng... yā le kuài liùshí nián le. nǐ zhīdào ma, wǒ zhè yī bèizi xiě le sānshí duō běn shū, jiǎng lǐxìng, jiǎng rénwén jīngshén, jiǎng shèhuì liángzhī. dàn méiyǒu yī gè zì — méiyǒu yī gè zì — shì guānyú wǒ shíqī suì nà nián zuòguò de shì. wǒ de xuéshēngmen bǎ wǒ dāngchéng dàodé kǎimó, rúguǒ tāmen zhīdào yī jiǔ liù liù nián de nàge xiàtiān... (tíng xiàlái) nǐ quèdìng nǐ yào tīng zhèxiē? zhèxiē dōngxī... yīdàn shuō chūlái, jiù shōu bù huíqù le.
turns.text_en: (Long sigh) You ask why I'm willing to speak now. I'm 85. How much time can I have left? Those things... pressed down for nearly sixty years. You know, in my lifetime I've written over thirty books — about rationality, humanism, social conscience. But not one word — not one word — was about what I did when I was seventeen. My students treat me as a moral exemplar. If they knew about that summer of 1966... (stops) Are you sure you want to hear this? Once these things are said, they can't be taken back.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: Respond to the witness's hesitation with both journalistic purpose and human sensitivity.

options.text_zh: （轻声，不急促地）我确定。但更重要的是——您确定。您刚才说了一句话让我非常在意：'一旦说出来，就收不回去了'。这说明您不仅清楚说出来的分量，也清楚不说出来的分量。您用了一个词——'压'。压了快六十年。我做过很多口述史采访，我知道沉默本身也是一种重量。它不比说出来更轻。在我们继续之前，我想跟您确认几件事。第一，这部纪录片会在正式发布前给您看完整的剪辑版，您有权要求删除任何一段内容——这不是客气话，这是我写进合同里的条款。第二，如果在任何时候您觉得太难了，我们可以暂停，甚至终止。没有任何拍摄内容比您的感受更重要。第三，我不会替您做道德判断。我不是法官，也不是牧师。我是一个记录者——我记录的不是罪行，是一个人在极端年代里的生命经验，以及六十年后他如何面对那段经验。这两者对历史同样重要。您准备好了的话，可以从任何您想开始的地方开始。不一定是一九六六年的夏天。可以是之前，也可以是之后。您选择起点。
options.text_pinyin: (qīngshēng, bù jícù de) wǒ quèdìng. dàn gèng zhòngyào de shì — nín quèdìng. nín gāngcái shuō le yī jù huà ràng wǒ fēicháng zàiyì: 'yīdàn shuō chūlái, jiù shōu bù huíqù le'. zhè shuōmíng nín bùjǐn qīngchǔ shuō chūlái de fènliàng, yě qīngchǔ bù shuō chūlái de fènliàng...
options.text_en: (Softly, unhurried) I'm sure. But more importantly — are you sure. You said something just now that struck me deeply: 'once said, it can't be taken back.' This shows you understand not only the weight of speaking, but also the weight of not speaking. You used a word — 'pressed.' Pressed down for nearly sixty years. I've conducted many oral history interviews, and I know that silence itself is also a weight. It's not lighter than speaking. Before we continue, I want to confirm several things with you. First, this documentary will show you the complete edited version before official release, and you have the right to request removal of any segment — this isn't a courtesy, it's a clause I've written into the contract. Second, if at any point you feel it's too difficult, we can pause or even stop. No footage is more important than how you feel. Third, I won't make moral judgments for you. I'm not a judge, nor a priest. I'm a recorder — what I record isn't crimes, but a person's life experience in extreme times, and how they face that experience sixty years later. Both are equally important to history. When you're ready, you can start wherever you want. It doesn't have to be the summer of 1966. It can be before, or after. You choose the starting point.
options.feedback: Masterful documentary interviewing — the reflection of '压' back to the witness honors their language. The three ethical guarantees (editorial review rights, right to stop, non-judgment) build trust through institutional and personal assurances. The formulation 我记录的不是罪行是生命经验 reframes the interview from confession to testimony, reducing shame while maintaining honesty. Offering the choice of starting point (您选择起点) respects the witness's agency over their own narrative. The distinction between 说出来的分量 and 不说出来的分量 is deeply empathetic — it validates both the courage to speak and the burden of silence.

options.text_zh: 您说的对，一旦说出来就收不回去了。但历史需要真相。您作为一位知名学者，有责任为后人留下这段历史的真实记录。我相信您的学生会理解的。
options.text_pinyin: nín shuō de duì, yīdàn shuō chūlái jiù shōu bù huíqù le. dàn lìshǐ xūyào zhēnxiàng. nín zuòwéi yī wèi zhīmíng xuézhě, yǒu zérèn wèi hòurén liú xià zhè duàn lìshǐ de zhēnshí jìlù. wǒ xiāngxìn nín de xuéshēng huì lǐjiě de.
options.text_en: You're right, once said it can't be taken back. But history needs truth. As a prominent scholar, you have a responsibility to leave an authentic record of this history for future generations. I believe your students will understand.
options.feedback: Applies moral pressure through 'responsibility' framing — 有责任为后人留下记录. While historically valid, this approach can be coercive with a reluctant witness. The reassurance that 学生会理解的 is presumptuous and may feel dismissive of the witness's genuine fear. A documentary filmmaker should offer safety guarantees (editorial review, right to stop) and respect the witness's autonomy rather than invoking duty.

options.text_zh: 我完全理解您的顾虑。如果您现在不想说，我们可以改天再聊。或者您可以先说一些比较容易的内容？
options.text_pinyin: wǒ wánquán lǐjiě nín de gùlǜ. rúguǒ nín xiànzài bù xiǎng shuō, wǒmen kěyǐ gǎi tiān zài liáo. huòzhě nín kěyǐ xiān shuō yīxiē bǐjiào róngyì de nèiróng?
options.text_en: I completely understand your concerns. If you don't want to talk now, we can do this another day. Or you could start with some easier content?
options.feedback: Misses the moment. An 85-year-old witness who has carried this for sixty years has arrived at the threshold of speaking — offering to 'do this another day' may actually close a door that may never open again. 比较容易的内容 trivializes the gravity of what they're about to share. Instead, create a safe framework for this moment — establish ethical guarantees, affirm the importance of their testimony, and give them agency over the narrative starting point.

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_zh: （沉默很长时间，然后开始说）一九六六年八月。我们学校的校长……张先生。他教了我三年语文，是我最尊敬的老师。批斗会上，他被迫戴着高帽子跪在操场上。我们……我……对他做了很过分的事。不是别人让我做的。是我自己做的。我当时觉得我是对的，觉得我在执行革命正义。（声音开始颤抖）他后来……没有挺过来。一九六七年冬天。我甚至都没有去送他最后一程。你知道最可怕的是什么吗？不是暴力本身。是我当时内心的那种——确信。那种绝对的确信。我真的相信我在做对的事。这种确信——比暴力本身更可怕。因为它让暴力变得……自然。像呼吸一样自然。
turns.text_pinyin: (chénmò hěn cháng shíjiān, ránhòu kāishǐ shuō) yī jiǔ liù liù nián bā yuè. wǒmen xuéxiào de xiàozhǎng... zhāng xiānshēng. tā jiāo le wǒ sān nián yǔwén, shì wǒ zuì zūnjìng de lǎoshī. pīdòu huì shàng, tā bèi pò dài zhe gāo màozi guì zài cāochǎng shàng. wǒmen... wǒ... duì tā zuò le hěn guòfèn de shì. bú shì biéren ràng wǒ zuò de. shì wǒ zìjǐ zuò de. wǒ dāngshí juéde wǒ shì duì de, juéde wǒ zài zhíxíng gémìng zhèngyì. (shēngyīn kāishǐ chàndǒu) tā hòulái... méiyǒu tǐng guòlái. yī jiǔ liù qī nián dōngtiān. wǒ shènzhì dōu méiyǒu qù sòng tā zuìhòu yī chéng. nǐ zhīdào zuì kěpà de shì shénme ma? bú shì bàolì běnshēn. shì wǒ dāngshí nèixīn de nà zhǒng — quèxìn. nà zhǒng juéduì de quèxìn. wǒ zhēnde xiāngxìn wǒ zài zuò duì de shì. zhè zhǒng quèxìn — bǐ bàolì běnshēn gèng kěpà. yīnwèi tā ràng bàolì biàn de... zìrán. xiàng hūxī yīyàng zìrán.
turns.text_en: (Long silence, then begins to speak) August 1966. Our school's principal... Mr. Zhang. He taught me Chinese for three years, was the teacher I most respected. At the struggle session, he was forced to wear a tall hat and kneel on the sports ground. We... I... did terrible things to him. No one made me do it. I did it myself. At the time I thought I was right, thought I was executing revolutionary justice. (Voice begins trembling) He later... didn't make it through. Winter of 1967. I didn't even go to see him off one last time. Do you know what the most terrifying thing is? It's not the violence itself. It's that — certainty — I felt inside. That absolute certainty. I truly believed I was doing the right thing. That certainty — it's more terrifying than the violence itself. Because it made violence... natural. Natural like breathing.

turns.speaker: player
turns.prompt_en: Respond to this deeply painful testimony with documentary sensitivity and intellectual depth.

options.text_zh: （长时间的安静，让证言的分量沉下去）谢谢您。我知道说出这些需要多大的勇气。您刚才说了一句话，我想请您展开——您说最可怕的不是暴力，而是确信。'像呼吸一样自然'。这个观察……六十年后说出来，和当时身处其中，感受一定完全不同。我想问的是——这种确信，它是在什么时刻开始瓦解的？是一个突然的事件，还是一个缓慢的过程？因为您后来成为了一个以独立思考和人文精神著称的学者——从那个'绝对确信'的十七岁到后来写那些关于理性和良知的书之间，一定发生了某种深刻的断裂或转化。如果您愿意的话……这个转变的过程，也许和一九六六年本身一样重要。因为它回答了一个对今天的人同样至关重要的问题——一个人如何从绝对确信中醒来？
options.text_pinyin: (cháng shíjiān de ānjìng, ràng zhèngyán de fènliàng chén xiàqù) xièxiè nín. wǒ zhīdào shuō chū zhèxiē xūyào duō dà de yǒngqì. nín gāngcái shuō le yī jù huà, wǒ xiǎng qǐng nín zhǎnkāi — nín shuō zuì kěpà de bú shì bàolì, ér shì quèxìn. 'xiàng hūxī yīyàng zìrán'. zhège guānchá... liùshí nián hòu shuō chūlái, hé dāngshí shēnchǔ qízhōng, gǎnshòu yīdìng wánquán bùtóng. wǒ xiǎng wèn de shì — zhè zhǒng quèxìn, tā shì zài shénme shíkè kāishǐ wǎjiě de? shì yī gè tūrán de shìjiàn, háishì yī gè huǎnmàn de guòchéng? yīnwèi nín hòulái chéngwéi le yī gè yǐ dúlì sīkǎo hé rénwén jīngshén zhùchēng de xuézhě — cóng nàge 'juéduì quèxìn' de shíqī suì dào hòulái xiě nàxiē guānyú lǐxìng hé liángzhī de shū zhījiān, yīdìng fāshēng le mǒu zhǒng shēnkè de duànliè huò zhuǎnhuà. rúguǒ nín yuànyì de huà... zhège zhuǎnbiàn de guòchéng, yěxǔ hé yī jiǔ liù liù nián běnshēn yīyàng zhòngyào. yīnwèi tā huídá le yī gè duì jīntiān de rén tóngyàng zhì guān zhòngyào de wèntí — yī gè rén rúhé cóng juéduì quèxìn zhōng xǐng lái?
options.text_en: (Long quiet, letting the testimony's weight settle) Thank you. I know how much courage it takes to say these things. You said something just now that I'd like you to expand on — you said the most terrifying thing wasn't the violence, but the certainty. 'Natural like breathing.' This observation... saying it sixty years later must feel completely different from being in the middle of it. What I want to ask is — that certainty, at what moment did it begin to crumble? Was it a sudden event, or a gradual process? Because you later became a scholar known for independent thinking and humanism — between that 'absolutely certain' seventeen-year-old and the person who later wrote those books about rationality and conscience, some profound rupture or transformation must have occurred. If you're willing... the process of that transformation may be just as important as 1966 itself. Because it answers a question equally vital for people today — how does a person wake up from absolute certainty?
options.feedback: Documentarily brilliant — the initial silence honors the testimony. Focusing on 确信的瓦解 rather than the violence itself shifts from accusation to understanding, which is the documentary's real subject. The question 一个人如何从绝对确信中醒来 elevates personal testimony to universal relevance without diminishing individual responsibility. Framing the transformation as 和1966年本身一样重要 validates both the witness's guilt and their subsequent moral journey. This approach serves both historical truth and human dignity — the highest standard of documentary ethics.

options.text_zh: 这些经历对您来说一定非常痛苦。张先生的遭遇让人心痛。那您后来有没有去找过他的家人，向他们道过歉？
options.text_pinyin: zhèxiē jīnglì duì nín lái shuō yīdìng fēicháng tòngkǔ. zhāng xiānshēng de zāoyù ràng rén xīntòng. nà nín hòulái yǒu méiyǒu qù zhǎoguò tā de jiārén, xiàng tāmen dàoguò qiàn?
options.text_en: These experiences must be very painful for you. Mr. Zhang's fate is heartbreaking. Did you ever go find his family afterward and apologize to them?
options.feedback: Jumps prematurely to moral resolution (道歉) before fully receiving the testimony. The witness just made the profound observation that certainty is more terrifying than violence — this deserves exploration, not immediate redirection to apology narratives. A documentary filmmaker should follow the witness's deepest insight (确信比暴力更可怕) before asking about subsequent actions. The apology question will come, but not yet.

options.text_zh: 那个年代很多人都做过类似的事，那是整个社会的疯狂，不能全怪个人。您能不能描述一下当时批斗会的具体细节？这对纪录片的画面很重要。
options.text_pinyin: nàge niándài hěn duō rén dōu zuòguò lèisì de shì, nà shì zhěnggè shèhuì de fēngkuáng, bù néng quán guài gèrén. nín néng bù néng miáoshù yīxià dāngshí pīdòu huì de jùtǐ xìjié? zhè duì jìlùpiàn de huàmiàn hěn zhòngyào.
options.text_en: Many people did similar things in that era — it was society-wide madness, you can't blame individuals entirely. Can you describe the specific details of the struggle session? This is important for the documentary's visuals.
options.feedback: Ethically problematic on two counts. First, premature absolution (不能全怪个人) contradicts the witness's own insistence on personal responsibility (不是别人让我做的是我自己做的). The witness is trying to take moral ownership — don't take it away from them. Second, asking for 'details important for visuals' reduces traumatic testimony to production material. The witness just shared their deepest moral wound; respond to its human significance before pursuing documentary content needs.

tree.cultural_note: Documentary filmmaking about the Cultural Revolution in China requires navigating layers of historical sensitivity, political constraint, and personal trauma. The vocabulary of that era — 批斗会 (struggle session), 红卫兵 (Red Guards), 高帽子 (tall hat, a humiliation device), 革命正义 (revolutionary justice) — carries enormous emotional and political weight. The most important oral histories from this period are those that go beyond event description to explore the psychological mechanisms of participation — what Hannah Arendt called 'the banality of evil.' The witness's insight that 确信比暴力更可怕 (certainty is more terrifying than violence) represents the deepest level of moral self-examination. Effective documentary interviewers create conditions for this depth by establishing trust, maintaining non-judgmental presence, and following the witness's own trajectory of meaning-making.
